description.en: The shortest of the major Caminos to Santiago de Compostela, walked since the medieval period by English, Irish, Scandinavian, and Flemish pilgrims who arrived by sea at the Galician ports of Ferrol and A Coruña. The standard route from Ferrol covers approximately 112 km along the Ría de Ferrol coast, through the medieval Gothic town of Betanzos, over the Bruma highlands, and down into Santiago. Modern revival began in earnest in the 2000s; in 2025 over 30,000 pilgrims walked it.
description.gl: O camiño máis curto a Santiago de Compostela polo norte, percorrido desde a Idade Media por peregrinos ingleses, irlandeses, escandinavos e flamengos que chegaban por mar aos portos galegos de Ferrol e A Coruña.
description.es: El más corto de los Caminos principales a Santiago de Compostela, recorrido desde la época medieval por peregrinos ingleses, irlandeses, escandinavos y flamencos que llegaban por mar a los puertos gallegos de Ferrol y La Coruña.

overview.distanceNote: Sum of 6-stage breakdown from Ferrol to Santiago, following Gronze.com's stage distances: Ferrol → Neda 12.4 km, Neda → Pontedeume 15.3 km, Pontedeume → Betanzos 19.7 km, Betanzos → Hospital de Bruma 24.0 km, Bruma → Sigüeiro 24.5 km, Sigüeiro → Santiago 15.7 km (total 111.6 km, rounded to 112). Matches OSM relation 1102966 distance tag (112 km). Cicerone's guidebook gives 116 km; Wikipedia cites 110 km — all within normal waymarking variance.

name.en: Curuxeiras pier, Ferrol (historic port of arrival)

origin.en: The Camino Inglés takes its name from medieval pilgrims who arrived by sea at the Galician ports of Ferrol and A Coruña, primarily from England, Ireland, Scandinavia, and Flanders. The route's peak use occurred during the Hundred Years' War (1337-1453), when English pilgrims and traders accessed Galician ports under royal license. The two historic starting cities — A Coruña (closer, ~75 km) and Ferrol (further, ~112 km) — diverge but converge at Hospital de Bruma. Modern pilgrims overwhelmingly start from Ferrol because A Coruña falls below the 100 km Compostela minimum walking distance.

description.en: Standard Camino credential, stamped (sello) at churches, albergues, cafés, and tourism offices along the route. Required for the Compostela certificate. Must be stamped at least twice per day for the qualifying distance. Since December 2016, pilgrims starting in A Coruña may qualify for the Compostela via the Celtic Camino program: pilgrims whose country of origin hosts a certified Jacobean pilgrimage route can walk a minimum 25 km on that home-country route before continuing from A Coruña. The program was created to address the historical inequity of the shorter A Coruña arrival point.

minimumDistanceNote.en: From Ferrol it is ~112 km to Santiago — comfortably above the 100 km Compostela minimum. From A Coruña it is only ~75 km, which does not qualify on its own; the December 2016 Celtic Camino rule change allows pilgrims to qualify by walking at least 25 km on a certified Jacobean pilgrimage route in their country of origin before continuing from A Coruña.

name.en: Concatedral de San Xulián de Ferrol
name.gl: Concatedral de San Xiao de Ferrol

description.en: Unlike the inland Caminos, the Camino Inglés begins at the sea. Medieval pilgrims arrived by ship at the Curuxeiras pier in Ferrol or at the port of A Coruña. The act of stepping off a boat onto the muelle and beginning the walk replaces the more common Camino opening of leaving a inland border town. Many modern pilgrims still touch the waters of the Ría de Ferrol or take a photograph at Curuxeiras before starting south.

A tiny hilltop hamlet where the historic Ferrol path meets the historic A Coruña path. Named for a medieval pilgrim hospital that served both routes. The albergue today sits at ~394 m, the highest point of the Camino Inglés. Pilgrims arriving from either start meet here on the climb out of the coastal plain.

Early 15th-century Gothic church in Betanzos built by Fernán Pérez de Andrade 'o Mozo' (the Younger — a descendant of the better-known Fernán Pérez de Andrade 'O Bo' who commissioned the nearby San Francisco in 1387). One of the 'Gothic trio' of Betanzos churches alongside Santa María del Azogue and San Francisco. The basilica plan with three naves features a Santiago Matamoros statue above the main portal. A traditional credential stamp point.

The scallop shell (vieira) is the universal symbol of the Camino. On the Camino Inglés, shell waymarkers are installed by the Xunta de Galicia in the standard blue-and-yellow Galician format. The route is well-marked throughout.

etiquette.en: Albergue capacity is significantly smaller than on the Francés. The Camino Inglés has fewer pilgrims and fewer beds — book ahead in peak season.
Galician is spoken alongside Spanish throughout the route. A few words of either language go far. Locals in small villages may default to Galician.
The Hospital de Bruma stage is long (~28 km) with limited services in between. Carry water and food, and start early.
Sunday markets in Betanzos (the second and fourth Sunday of each month) are a beloved local tradition; pilgrims passing through that day will find streets closed and stalls everywhere.

associatedLiterature.title: The Camino Inglés and Ruta do Mar
associatedLiterature.author: Dave Whitson, Laura Perazzoli

relevance.en: Cicerone Press dedicated guide (published 2019). Covers the Camino Inglés from both Ferrol and A Coruña starts, plus the Ruta do Mar maritime alternative. The most detailed English-language guidebook for this route.

associatedLiterature.title: A Pilgrim's Guide to the Camino Inglés
associatedLiterature.author: John Brierley

relevance.en: Kaminn Media (publisher of the Brierley Camino Guides since 2017). Brierley's signature format applied to the Camino Inglés: 1:200,000 maps, stage-by-stage spiritual reflections, practical logistics. Defines a 6-stage breakdown from Ferrol used by many English-speaking pilgrims.

description.en: The patron saint's feast day. Special masses at Santiago Cathedral, fireworks, and cultural events throughout the city. When July 25 falls on a Sunday, it is declared a Holy Year (Año Xacobeo). Next Holy Year: 2027.

Riverboat festival held in Betanzos on two fixed dates each August: the 18th and the 25th. Pilgrims passing through during these days encounter a town in full festive mode, with decorated boats sailing along the Mandeo river to the nearby Os Caneiros meadow. The festival is part of the broader Betanzos August celebrations.

historicalSignificance.en: The Camino Inglés is the most distinctively maritime of the major Caminos. Unlike the Francés or Norte, which begin at inland border crossings, the Inglés begins where pilgrims stepped off boats — Ferrol's Curuxeiras pier and A Coruña's old harbor. From the 12th century onwards, English, Irish, Scandinavian, and Flemish pilgrims used the route as a 'path of convenience' that avoided the long overland journey through France. Use peaked during the Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) when English kings issued royal licenses for Santiago pilgrimage despite the conflict with France. The route was largely forgotten after the Reformation reduced English Catholic pilgrimage in the 16th-17th centuries, then revived in the late 20th century. In 2025, 30,204 pilgrims walked it (5.7% of all Compostelas) — small relative to Frances (45.6%) but among the fastest-growing major Caminos in percentage terms. The Camino Inglés is NOT UNESCO inscribed, unlike the Francés (1993), Norte, Primitivo, and Lebaniego (all added in the 2015 Northern Spain extension).

notes.en: Open year-round with no seasonal closures. Galicia has a wet Atlantic climate — expect rain in any month. The high point at Hospital de Bruma (~394 m) can be cold and exposed in winter; April-October is most popular. Albergues outside the high season may have reduced services.

accommodationNotes.en: The Camino Inglés has significantly fewer beds than Frances or Portugués. Public albergues exist in Pontedeume, Betanzos, Hospital de Bruma, and Sigüeiro. Private pensiones and casas rurais fill gaps. In peak season (July-August) booking ahead is strongly recommended — especially Hospital de Bruma which has only ~22 beds and is the only accommodation at that altitude.

cellularNotes.en: Mobile coverage is generally good throughout. The Hospital de Bruma highland section may have weaker signal.

waterNotes.en: Fountains (fontes/fuentes) are common in Galician villages. Tap water is potable throughout Galicia unless marked 'auga non potable' (gl) or 'agua no potable' (es).

notes.en: Closest major airport, ~7 km from A Coruña and ~45 km from Ferrol. Domestic and limited European connections.
About 12 km from Santiago de Compostela, in Lavacolla. Direct flights to many European cities. Bus connections to Santiago every 30 minutes; ~95 km by road from Ferrol.
Standard-gauge Renfe trains run from Santiago and A Coruña to Ferrol. There is no direct standard-gauge train from Santiago to Ferrol — pilgrims normally route via A Coruña (~1h15).

The historic FEVE narrow-gauge (1,000 mm) runs from Bilbao via Santander, Oviedo, and the Cantabrian coast to Ferrol. Operated as a series of regional services rather than a single through-train. Atmospheric option for pilgrims arriving from northern Spain.
Direct bus service Santiago to Ferrol via the AP-9 motorway, ~1h45. Often the easiest way back to Santiago after completing the Camino if returning to fly out of SCQ.
